9.notes:
Ecclesiastes - Chapter 9
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The vanity of death's universality — the same fate (death) comes to all, righteous and wicked alike; therefore enjoy life with your wife, work heartily, for there is no knowledge or work in Sheol where you are going.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: 'The Preacher' (Qoheleth), traditionally identified as Solomon.
Written late in life as a reflection on life's meaning. Hebrew title:
'Qoheleth' — 'The Assembler/Preacher.' Key themes: the vanity (hebel =
vapor/breath) of life 'under the sun' (without God's perspective), the
search for meaning in pleasure/wisdom/work/wealth, the certainty of
death, and the conclusion: 'Fear God, and keep his commandments: for
this is the whole duty of man' (12:13).

Historical Context: Chapter 9 confronts the great equalizer: death.
The righteous and the wicked, the clean and the unclean, share "one
event" — they all die (vv.1-3). This observation leads not to
nihilism but to the book's most passionate enjoyment passage
(vv.7-10): since death is certain, live fully now — eat with joy,
dress festively, love your spouse, and do your work with all your
might. The urgency is palpable: "there is no work, nor device, nor
knowledge, nor wisdom, in the grave, whither thou goest" (v.10).
The chapter then turns to the role of "time and chance" (v.11) —
outcomes are not always proportional to ability — and concludes with
a parable about a poor wise man who saves a city but is forgotten
(vv.13-18). The message: wisdom is superior to strength and weapons,
yet it is despised and unrewarded in this world. Live faithfully
regardless.

Structure:
- Death: The One Event for All (vv.1-6)
- The Passionate Call to Live Fully (vv.7-10)
- Time and Chance: Outcomes Are Unpredictable (vv.11-12)
- The Parable of the Poor Wise Man (vv.13-18)

CROSS-REFERENCES

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/ecclesiastes-9/
  Guzik notes that vv.7-10 constitute "the most energetic and
  passionate of all the 'enjoyment passages'" in Ecclesiastes.
  Solomon moves from observation to command: Go! Eat! Drink!
  Work! Live! The awareness of death (vv.1-6) does not produce
  despair but urgency — if life is brief, then waste none of it.
  The parable of the poor wise man (vv.13-18) teaches that
  wisdom's value is intrinsic, not dependent on recognition.
  Do the right thing even when no one notices or remembers.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Whatsoever thy hand findeth to do, do it with thy might.
  Half-hearted work is hardly work at all. God calls no man to
  serve Him with a lukewarm spirit. Whether you preach, or
  teach, or dig ditches, or tend children — do it with the whole
  force of your being. The grave will give you rest enough. While
  you live, live! Let every faculty be stretched for the glory of
  God. You have but one life — spend it; do not merely save it."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "no man knoweth either love or hatred by all that is before them" / ESV: "whether it is love or hate, man does not know" — The Hebrew is ambiguous: either (a) we cannot tell if God loves or hates us based on external circumstances, or (b) we cannot predict what lies ahead. Both readings reinforce the same point: outcomes do not reliably indicate God's disposition toward us.
v.4              | KJV         | "a living dog is better than a dead lion" / ESV same — A proverb using animals of opposite status. Dogs were despised scavengers in ancient Israel; lions were symbols of royalty and power. Yet a living dog surpasses a dead lion — because the living still have possibility. Hope belongs only to the living.
v.11             | KJV         | "time and chance" / ESV: "time and chance" — Hebrew "et vapha." "Pega'" = occurrence, happening, incident. From the human perspective, unforeseen events disrupt expected outcomes. From God's perspective, nothing is random — but we experience it as such.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Time and Chance (et vapega') | The convergence of a particular moment with an unforeseen event. Not randomness in the philosophical sense (God remains sovereign) but unpredictability from the human vantage point. Outcomes cannot be guaranteed by human ability alone. | 
One Event (miqreh echad) | Death — the single destiny shared by all humanity regardless of moral character, wisdom, or achievement. The great equalizer that renders all earthly distinctions temporary. | 
Portion (cheleq)  | One's allotted share in life. In v.9, marriage, daily work, and simple joys constitute one's "portion" — the life God has given to be lived fully rather than anxiously hoarded or carelessly wasted. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Death makes all earthly distinctions temporary (vv.2-3). The righteous
   and the wicked share one destination physically. This should humble you —
   your advantages are temporary. It should also motivate you: live now for
   what outlasts the grave.

2. Live with holy urgency (v.10). You do not have unlimited time. Whatever
   God has given you to do — do it now, with full force. The grave offers no
   second chances, no do-overs, no makeup assignments. Today is the day for
   action.

3. Receive daily life as God's gift with joyful abandon (vv.7-9). Eat with
   joy. Dress for celebration. Love your spouse. God accepts your works —
   you have His approval to live fully. Joyless duty-keeping is not what He
   asks. He asks for wholehearted, grateful, joyful living.

4. Do not assume outcomes match ability (v.11). The race is not always to
   the swift. Work hard, develop your gifts, pursue wisdom — but do not
   presume that effort guarantees results. Hold outcomes loosely. Time and
   chance (under God's sovereign hand) determine what you cannot control.

5. Do what is wise even if no one notices (vv.15-16). The poor wise man
   saved the city and was forgotten. This will happen to you. Do the right
   thing anyway. Wisdom's value is intrinsic, not dependent on human
   recognition. God sees; that is enough.

10.notes:
Ecclesiastes - Chapter 10
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Practical wisdom observations — folly is like dead flies in ointment; a little foolishness outweighs wisdom; the Preacher offers proverbs on rulers, laziness, speech, and the unpredictability of life.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: 'The Preacher' (Qoheleth), traditionally identified as Solomon.
Written late in life as a reflection on life's meaning. Hebrew title:
'Qoheleth' — 'The Assembler/Preacher.' Key themes: the vanity (hebel =
vapor/breath) of life 'under the sun' (without God's perspective), the
search for meaning in pleasure/wisdom/work/wealth, the certainty of
death, and the conclusion: 'Fear God, and keep his commandments: for
this is the whole duty of man' (12:13).

Historical Context: Chapter 10 is the most "proverbial" chapter in
Ecclesiastes — a collection of wisdom sayings about folly, rulers,
work, and speech that closely resembles the style of Proverbs. After
the weighty existential reflections of chapters 1-9, the Preacher
turns to practical wisdom for daily life. The chapter opens with the
famous image of dead flies contaminating perfume (v.1) — a small
amount of folly ruins a great reputation. The chapter addresses
governance (vv.4-7, 16-17, 20), the hazards of work (vv.8-10), the
danger of foolish speech (vv.11-15), and the consequences of laziness
(v.18). Though less philosophical than earlier chapters, these
observations serve the book's larger purpose: since life is hebel
and we cannot control ultimate outcomes, at least live with practical
wisdom in the daily affairs we can influence.

Structure:
- The Contaminating Power of Folly (vv.1-3)
- Wisdom Under Authority (vv.4-7)
- The Hazards of Work and the Value of Skill (vv.8-11)
- The Fool's Speech vs. the Wise Man's Words (vv.12-15)
- Good and Bad Governance (vv.16-20)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | 1 Corinthians | 5       | 6                  | "A little leaven leavens the whole lump." (ESV) Small corruption spreads throughout
v.1              | Galatians   | 5       | 9                  | "A little leaven leavens the whole lump." (ESV) Paul uses the same principle
v.4              | Proverbs    | 15      | 1                  | "A soft answer turns away wrath." (ESV) The power of gentle response
v.8              | Proverbs    | 26      | 27                 | "Whoever digs a pit will fall into it." (ESV) Actions rebound on their authors
v.10             | Proverbs    | 21      | 5                  | "The plans of the diligent lead surely to abundance." (ESV) Preparation pays dividends
v.12             | Proverbs    | 10      | 32                 | "The lips of the righteous know what is acceptable." (ESV) Wise speech finds favor
v.16             | Isaiah      | 3       | 4-5                | "I will make boys their princes." (ESV) Immature leadership as divine judgment
v.18             | Proverbs    | 24      | 30-34              | The field of the slothful man — overgrown and ruined through neglect

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/ecclesiastes-10/
  Guzik observes that v.1 establishes the chapter's theme: the
  disproportionate power of small folly. "It takes much wisdom
  to build a good reputation and only a little folly to destroy
  it." On v.10, Guzik notes the practical genius of Solomon's
  observation: wisdom is not merely philosophical — it is the
  difference between grinding through life with a dull axe and
  working effectively with a sharp one. "Sharpen the axe" is
  still wisdom for daily life: prepare, think, plan — then act.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Dead flies in the ointment — what a picture! One foolish word,
  one rash act, one moment of self-indulgence, and a lifetime of
  faithful service is tarnished. How careful we ought to be! The
  enemy of our souls does not need a great scandal to ruin us —
  a 'little folly' will serve his purpose. Guard the small
  things. It is the little foxes that spoil the vines."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "Dead flies" / ESV: "Dead flies" — Hebrew "zebubei mavet" literally = "flies of death." The image is vivid: insects that have died and decomposed in the precious ointment, causing it to putrefy and stink. The plural "flies" is sometimes rendered as a singular principle — even one dead fly is sufficient to ruin the perfume.
v.10             | KJV         | "wisdom is profitable to direct" / ESV: "wisdom helps one to succeed" — Hebrew "yithron haksher chokmah" = the advantage/profit of making wise/straight is wisdom. The verse is compressed but the meaning is clear: wisdom (preparation, skill, forethought) gives the decisive advantage in all labor.
v.11             | KJV         | "a babbler is no better" / ESV: "the charmer has no advantage" — Hebrew "ba'al halashon" literally = "master of the tongue." The image connects to the serpent: if you do not charm the snake before it bites, your ability as a charmer is useless. Timing matters — wisdom applied too late is no wisdom at all.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Ointment (shemen) | Oil, perfume, precious aromatic ointment. Perfume was expensive and laboriously prepared in the ancient world. It represents accumulated reputation, honor, or wisdom — something of great value that is easily contaminated by a small impurity. | 
Yielding (marpe') | Healing, gentleness, calmness, composure. From the root "rapha" (to heal). In v.4, it is the quality that defuses a ruler's anger — not weakness but controlled gentleness. The same word elsewhere describes healing medicine. | 
Whet (chadad)     | To sharpen, make keen. In v.10, it describes sharpening an axe blade — preparation that makes subsequent work more effective. Metaphorically: any investment in preparation, training, or planning that multiplies the productivity of effort. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Guard against "little" follies (v.1). A lifetime of wisdom can be undone
   by a single foolish act. The dead fly is small; the ruin is total. Watch
   the small decisions, the unguarded moments, the careless words. It is not
   the spectacular sins that destroy most people but the little ones.

2. Stay calm under pressure (v.4). When authority turns against you, do not
   panic or flee. Composure and gentleness can defuse situations that
   reactive anger would inflame. Self-control in a crisis reveals (and
   builds) true character.

3. Sharpen the axe before swinging harder (v.10). If your efforts are
   producing little fruit, the answer may not be more effort but better
   preparation. Study, plan, train, pray — then work. Wisdom makes labor
   effective; raw effort alone grinds inefficiently.

4. Guard your tongue — and your thoughts (vv.12-14, 20). Foolish speech is
   self-destructive. Even private complaints and secret thoughts tend to
   surface. Live as though every word — spoken or thought — will eventually
   be heard. Because it will be (Matthew 12:36).

5. Small neglect leads to great ruin (v.18). "By much slothfulness the
   building decayeth." The house does not collapse in a day but through
   sustained neglect. Spiritual life, relationships, health, finances — all
   decay through inattention. Maintain what matters before it requires
   rebuilding.

3. If the clouds are full of rain, they will empty themselves on the earth, and whether a tree falls to the south or to the north, the tree will lie wherever it falls.
4. He who watches the wind will not sow, and he who observes the clouds will not reap.
5. Just as you do not know the path of the wind or how the bones form in the womb of a pregnant woman, so you do not know the work of God who makes everything.
6. Sow your seed in the morning, and do not stop working until the evening; for you do not know which activity will succeed— whether this one or that one, or whether both will prosper equally.
7. Light is sweet, and it is pleasant for a person to see the sun.
8. So, if a man lives many years, let him rejoice in them all, but let him remember that the days of darkness will be many—all that is about to come is obscure.
9. Rejoice, young man, while you are young, and let your heart cheer you in the days of your youth. Follow the impulses of your heart and the desires of your eyes, but know that God will judge your motives and actions.
10. Banish emotional stress from your mind and put away pain from your body; for youth and the prime of life are fleeting.

11.notes:
Ecclesiastes - Chapter 11
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The call to bold, joyful action — cast your bread upon the waters, sow in morning and evening since you cannot know which will prosper; rejoice in youth but remember judgment is coming.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: 'The Preacher' (Qoheleth), traditionally identified as Solomon.
Written late in life as a reflection on life's meaning. Hebrew title:
'Qoheleth' — 'The Assembler/Preacher.' Key themes: the vanity (hebel =
vapor/breath) of life 'under the sun' (without God's perspective), the
search for meaning in pleasure/wisdom/work/wealth, the certainty of
death, and the conclusion: 'Fear God, and keep his commandments: for
this is the whole duty of man' (12:13).

Historical Context: Chapter 11 marks the Preacher's final practical
counsel before the book's concluding meditation on aging and death
(ch.12). Having established that life is hebel, that outcomes are
unpredictable, and that death awaits all, the Preacher now draws the
practical conclusion: act boldly despite uncertainty. "Cast thy bread
upon the waters" (v.1) — invest generously even when returns are not
guaranteed. "In the morning sow thy seed, and in the evening withhold
not thine hand" (v.6) — work diligently without demanding certainty
of results. The chapter's final section (vv.7-10) turns to youth
with urgency: rejoice while you can, walk in the ways of your heart,
but remember — God will judge. "Childhood and youth are vanity"
(v.10) — they too are fleeting. This sets up chapter 12's command
to "remember now thy Creator in the days of thy youth." The tone
shifts from philosophical inquiry to pastoral urgency: time is short;
act now.

Structure:
- Bold Generosity Despite Uncertainty (vv.1-2)
- Act Without Demanding Certainty (vv.3-6)
- Rejoice in Youth — But Remember Judgment (vv.7-10)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Galatians   | 6       | 9                  | "And let us not grow weary of doing good, for in due season we will reap, if we do not give up." (ESV) Generous effort brings eventual return
v.1              | Luke        | 6       | 38                 | "Give, and it will be given to you." (ESV) The principle of generous return
v.2              | Luke        | 12      | 33                 | "Sell your possessions, and give to the needy." (ESV) Generous distribution of resources
v.4              | James       | 1       | 6                  | "But let him ask in faith, with no doubting." (ESV) Act without paralyzing doubt
v.5              | John        | 3       | 8                  | "The wind blows where it wishes...so it is with everyone who is born of the Spirit." (ESV) God's ways are mysterious, like the wind
v.6              | 2 Corinthians | 9       | 6                  | "Whoever sows bountifully will also reap bountifully." (ESV)
v.9              | 2 Corinthians | 5       | 10                 | "For we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ." (ESV) Accountability for all
v.9              | Romans      | 14      | 12                 | "So then each of us will give an account of himself to God." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/ecclesiastes-11/
  Guzik emphasizes that vv.1-6 are Ecclesiastes' answer to
  paralysis: since you cannot control outcomes (ch.9) and
  cannot understand God's ways (ch.8), the wise response is not
  inaction but bold, diversified effort. "Cast thy bread" means
  venture forth — take the risk. Waiting for certainty means
  never acting. On v.9, Guzik notes that the command to rejoice
  is not sarcasm or permission for sin — it is a genuine
  endorsement of youthful joy paired with a genuine warning
  about accountability. Both halves are equally serious.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Cast thy bread upon the waters — there it seems lost, sunk,
  gone forever. But after many days, thou shalt find it. So it
  is with every act of faith and generosity. The seed sown in
  tears, the gift given when you could barely afford it, the
  kindness shown to one who could never repay — these are not
  lost. God keeps perfect accounts. What seems wasted on the
  waters is being carried by divine currents to the shore of
  eternal reward."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "Cast thy bread upon the waters" / ESV: "Cast your bread upon the waters" — Hebrew "shalach lachmeka al-penei hammayim." The idiom likely originated from maritime commerce (sending grain overseas by ship) but came to mean any bold, generous venture into uncertainty. "Bread" = your substance, your livelihood, your investment.
v.5              | KJV         | "the way of the spirit" / ESV: "the way the spirit comes to the bones" — Hebrew "ruach" can mean spirit, wind, or breath. The verse may refer to the mystery of (a) the wind's path, (b) the spirit entering an unborn child, or (c) both — using the double meaning to illustrate God's inscrutable ways.
v.9              | KJV         | "walk in the ways of thine heart" / ESV: "walk in the ways of your heart" — Not an endorsement of following sinful desires but an affirmation that youthful energy and joy are God-given. The corrective immediately follows: "God will bring thee into judgment." Enjoy responsibly; you are accountable.
v.10             | KJV         | "childhood and youth are vanity" / ESV: "youth and the dawn of life are vanity" — Hebrew "yaldut v'shacharut" = childhood and the black-haired stage (youth/dawn). Even the most vibrant season of life is hebel — fleeting as breath. This motivates urgency, not despair.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Cast (shalach)    | To send, dispatch, stretch out, release. In v.1, it describes the bold release of one's resources into uncertain circumstances. Not a careless throw but a deliberate venture — sending bread upon the waters with expectation of eventual return. Faith in action. | 
Vanity of youth (hebel) | Even youth — the strongest, most vibrant season of life — is fleeting. This is not cynicism but realism: the days of energy and opportunity are shorter than they appear. Use them wisely before they pass. | 
Seven and eight   | In v.2, "Give a portion to seven, and also to eight" uses the "X and X+1" poetic formula (cf. Amos 1:3) to mean: diversify generously, beyond what seems sufficient. Do not hold back or concentrate all investment in one place. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Act boldly despite uncertainty (v.1). You will never have perfect
   information. You will never be guaranteed success. Cast your bread
   anyway. Give generously. Start the venture. Make the phone call. Write
   the letter. Faith acts without demanding certainty.

2. Do not let fear of imperfect conditions paralyze you (v.4). The wind will
   never be perfectly still. The clouds will never perfectly clear. If you
   wait for ideal circumstances, you will wait forever. Sow in imperfect
   conditions — God controls the harvest regardless.

3. Diversify your faithful efforts (v.6). You do not know which endeavor
   will bear fruit. Sow morning and evening — give, serve, invest, work in
   multiple ways. Let God determine which seeds prosper. Your job is
   faithful sowing, not guaranteed outcomes.

4. Enjoy your youth — but remember accountability (v.9). Joy in your
   strength and energy is God-given, not sinful. But every joy exists under
   God's judgment. Freedom is not license. Rejoice fully and live
   responsibly — these are not contradictions but companions.

5. Youth is fleeting; do not waste it (v.10). Remove anxiety ("sorrow from
   thy heart") and remove sin ("evil from thy flesh") — both steal the brief
   days of vitality. Time spent in worry or in wickedness is time you cannot
   recover. Act now while strength remains.

------------------------------------

12.notes:
Ecclesiastes - Chapter 12
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The conclusion — remember your Creator in youth before the allegorical description of old age and death; 'vanity of vanities, all is vanity'; the final verdict: fear God and keep His commandments, for God will judge every deed.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: 'The Preacher' (Qoheleth), traditionally identified as Solomon.
Written late in life as a reflection on life's meaning. Hebrew title:
'Qoheleth' — 'The Assembler/Preacher.' Key themes: the vanity (hebel =
vapor/breath) of life 'under the sun' (without God's perspective), the
search for meaning in pleasure/wisdom/work/wealth, the certainty of
death, and the conclusion: 'Fear God, and keep his commandments: for
this is the whole duty of man' (12:13).

Historical Context: Chapter 12 is the grand conclusion of Ecclesiastes
— one of the most powerful and poetic passages in all Scripture. It
begins with the urgent command "Remember now thy Creator in the days
of thy youth" (v.1), continuing directly from chapter 11's address to
the young. Verses 1-7 contain an extended allegorical poem describing
old age and death through a series of metaphors: the darkening of
the sun and stars (failing eyesight), trembling keepers of the house
(shaking arms), strong men bowing (legs weakening), grinders ceasing
(teeth falling out), doors shutting in the streets (deafness), the
almond tree flourishing (white hair), and the grasshopper becoming a
burden (frailty). The poem culminates in death: "the dust return to
the earth as it was: and the spirit shall return unto God who gave
it" (v.7). The epilogue (vv.8-14) steps outside the Preacher's voice
to summarize his work and deliver the book's final verdict: "Fear
God, and keep his commandments: for this is the whole duty of man"
(v.13). This is the answer to every question the book has raised.

Structure:
- Remember Your Creator Before Old Age (vv.1-2)
- The Allegory of Aging and Death (vv.3-7)
- The Epilogue: The Preacher's Summary (vv.8-12)
- The Conclusion of the Whole Matter (vv.13-14)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Lamentations | 3       | 27                 | "It is good for a man that he bear the yoke in his youth." (ESV) Early devotion to God
v.1              | 2 Timothy   | 3       | 15                 | "From childhood you have been acquainted with the sacred writings." (ESV) Timothy's early faith
v.7              | Genesis     | 2       | 7                  | "Then the LORD God formed the man of dust from the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life." (ESV) Creation's order reversed in death
v.7              | Genesis     | 3       | 19                 | "For you are dust, and to dust you shall return." (ESV) The curse fulfilled
v.11             | Hebrews     | 4       | 12                 | "For the word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword." (ESV) Words as goads
v.13             | Deuteronomy | 10      | 12                 | "What does the LORD your God require of you, but to fear the LORD your God." (ESV) The same duty
v.13             | Micah       | 6       | 8                  | "What does the LORD require of you?" (ESV) The same question answered similarly
v.14             | Matthew     | 12      | 36                 | "On the day of judgment people will give account for every careless word they speak." (ESV) Comprehensive judgment
v.14             | 2 Corinthians | 5       | 10                 | "For we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ." (ESV) NT fulfillment

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/ecclesiastes-12/
  Guzik calls the allegory of aging (vv.1-7) "one of the most
  remarkable poetic passages in all the Bible." Each image
  corresponds to physical decline: arms tremble (v.3a), legs
  weaken (v.3b), teeth fail (v.3c), eyes dim (v.3d), hearing
  fades (v.4), fear of heights increases (v.5), desire fails
  (v.5). On vv.13-14, Guzik writes: "After all the searching,
  questioning, investigating, testing, and concluding — this is
  the answer. Not pleasure, not wisdom, not work, not wealth.
  Fear God and obey Him. This is the whole duty — and the whole
  dignity — of man."

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Remember now thy Creator in the days of thy youth. Now — not
  when your knees tremble and your hair whitens. Now — not when
  the world has wrung you dry and cast you off. Now — while your
  heart is tender, your mind sharp, your will strong. Give God
  the morning of your life, not merely the evening. He who has
  your youth has all of you. He who receives only your old age
  receives what the world has already used and discarded. Fear
  God NOW — this is the whole of man."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "Remember now thy Creator" / ESV: "Remember also your Creator" — Hebrew "bore'eka" = your Creator (plural of majesty). Some ancient manuscripts read "bore'ka" (your well/cistern = wife, cf. Proverbs 5:15), but the standard reading "Creator" is overwhelmingly supported and fits the book's theological conclusion perfectly.
v.3              | KJV         | The allegorical images: "keepers of the house" = arms/hands; "strong men" = legs; "grinders" = teeth; "those that look out of the windows" = eyes. Each describes the progressive deterioration of the body in old age. The beauty of the poetry lies in never naming the reality directly.
v.5              | KJV         | "the almond tree shall flourish" / ESV: "the almond tree blossoms" — The almond tree blooms white — representing white hair. "The grasshopper shall be a burden" = even the lightest thing becomes heavy to the aged. "Desire shall fail" — Hebrew "aviyyonah" may mean desire (caperberry as aphrodisiac) or the caperberry itself fails.
v.6              | KJV         | "the silver cord" / ESV: "the silver cord" — Four images of death: the silver cord (spinal cord or life- thread), the golden bowl (the skull or lamp of life), the pitcher at the fountain (the heart), the wheel at the cistern (circulatory system). All describe life's mechanism breaking irreparably.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Creator (bore')   | The One who formed and made you. From "bara'" (to create). The specific choice of "Creator" (rather than "God" or "Lord") in v.1 is significant: remember that you are a creature, not self-made. You owe your existence to Another, and to Him you are accountable. | 
Conclusion (soph) | End, sum, totality. In v.13, "the conclusion of the whole matter" = the final word, the sum total, the bottom line. After twelve chapters of investigation, this is the result: fear God and obey. Everything else in the book points here. | 
Whole duty (kol-ha'adam) | Literally "the whole of man" or "all of man." Not merely one duty among many but the totality of what it means to be human. To fear God and keep His commandments is to fulfill human existence completely. This is what you were made for. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Give God your youth, not your leftovers (v.1). The command is urgent:
   "Remember now." Not when you retire. Not when you have more time. Not
   when the world disappoints you enough to try God. Now — while your
   strength is full, your mind is sharp, your years stretch before you. God
   deserves the first and best, not the last and least.

2. Your body will fail (vv.3-5). This is not pessimism but reality. Arms
   will tremble, legs will weaken, teeth will fail, eyes will dim, hearing
   will fade. Prepare your soul now for the body's decline. The inner life
   must be strong enough to carry you when the outer life fails. Invest in
   what aging cannot touch.

3. Death is certain — but the spirit returns to God (v.7). The body returns
   to dust; the spirit returns to its Maker. You are not merely physical.
   Death is not annihilation but separation — and for the God-fearer, it is
   a homecoming. Live today in light of that return.

4. Fear God and keep His commandments (v.13). After all the questions, all
   the experiments, all the observations — this is the answer. Not
   complicated. Not sophisticated. Fear God. Obey Him. This is the whole of
   what it means to be human. When everything else is stripped away, this
   remains.

5. Every secret thing will face judgment (v.14). Nothing is hidden from God.
   No thought, no motive, no private act escapes His evaluation. This is
   both terrifying and liberating: terrifying because nothing can be
   concealed; liberating because nothing good — however unnoticed by others
   — is wasted before God. Live for the audience of One.
